text: Rapport
Small talk
Egalitarian
Cordiality
Punctuality
Formality
Understatement
Code-switching
Etiquette
Reciprocity
A question about the weekend is how you break the ice here, and the meeting goes better for it.
I have to read the room, not just the words, because the real objection is rarely said out loud.
I hit it off with the operations lead over coffee, and that one conversation opened three doors.
I raised the problem privately so the coordinator could save face, and she fixed it the same day.
The unwritten rules are where the real culture lives, and they are exactly what no document ever gives you.
I wish the team made more small talk before we start, but they do not, and I am adapting.
I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one, because I read the formality completely wrong.
I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement. When I go quiet, I am thinking.
If only I had read the room, I would have caught the objection behind the word interesting.
I wish the objections in our reviews were more direct, because I sometimes miss the understatement.
So I am learning to read the room, and I treat interesting as a signal to ask one more question.
And when you do have a real concern, I would genuinely rather you told me plainly. It helps me help you.
Lead with the wish, not the verdict, and you are a leader adapting. Lead with the verdict, and you are a newcomer resisting.
So, first week done. Be honest with me, how did it actually feel? Not the diplomatic version.

Honestly? Humbling. I wish I had asked you about the etiquette before I walked in. On Monday I opened with the agenda, and I could feel the room cool down. In Brazil that reads as respecting people's time; here it read as cold.
Everyone does that in week one. The small talk feels like a delay until you realize it is the whole point. What else caught you off guard?

The understatement. In Thursday's review, someone said my plan was interesting and not quite there yet, and I took it as approval. I wish I had read the room instead of the words. If only I had asked one follow-up question, I would have caught the objection.

And there is one I want to change, not just survive. I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement. When I go quiet I am weighing something, not saying yes. So I have started saying let me think on that out loud; it saves face for everyone and it keeps things clear. I am not fighting the culture, Nathan. I am code-switching into it.

That right there is the difference. Most people spend month one telling us what is wrong with us. You just told me what you wish were different and what you are already doing about it; that is a leader reading the unwritten rules, not a newcomer resisting them. Give it a month. You are going to hit it off with this team.
I wish the meetings got to the point faster, but the small talk is where the rapport is built, so I break the ice first.
I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one, because I read the formality of this office wrong.
I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement, so I now say let me think on that out loud.
If only I had read the room, I would have caught the understatement behind the word interesting.
The reciprocity here is real: I invest in the small talk first, and the team invests back. I am code-switching, not resisting.

Danielle, it is Nathan, from People and Culture here in Toronto. I am recording this before your first full week, because there is a whole layer of this office that will not be in any onboarding document, and I wish somebody had recorded something like this for me when I moved the other way, from Vancouver into a team that ran on rules nobody had ever written down. So here are three of them. First, the small talk is not filler. In Brazil you told me people warm up fast and get personal quickly; here it is the opposite on the surface. People will spend five minutes on the weekend and the weather before anyone says a real thing, and if you skip that to be efficient, you do not read as focused, you read as cold. The small talk is how we break the ice, and it is where the rapport is actually built. Second, we are flatter than you expect. Our VP is called by her first name, she lines up for coffee like everyone else, and if you defer to her too visibly in a meeting, people find it a little uncomfortable, not respectful. It is an egalitarian style, and it takes some getting used to. And third, watch the understatement. When a Canadian says something is not quite there yet, or that it is interesting, that is often a real objection wearing a polite coat. Learn to read the room, because the disagreement is in the room, it is just not on the table. None of this means you change who you are. It means you code-switch, the same way you already do between Portuguese and English. Give it a month. You will hit it off with this team faster than you think.

Danielle, it is Ruth, your coach. You sent me a long message that was basically a list of things that frustrate you about the Canadian office, and I want to do something useful with it, because the frustration is not the problem. The problem is that it has nowhere to go. So here is the tool for this week. Every one of those frustrations, say it to yourself starting with I wish. Not to complain, but to name precisely what you would change. And notice what the grammar forces you to do. When you say I wish the team made more small talk, you are not describing reality, so English pulls the verb back into the past, made, even though you mean now. That little backward step is the language admitting this is not how things are, this is how I would want them. Then there is the sharper one, the regret. When you say I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one, the had asked is you looking back and wanting a different past, and that is the most senior thing you can do with a mistake, name it without drowning in it. And when it is other people you want to change, I wish they would stop reading my silence as agreement, the would carries the wanting. Here is why this matters more than it looks. A leader who only says this is wrong sounds like she is resisting the culture. A leader who says I wish it worked differently, and here is what I am doing meanwhile, sounds like she is adapting to it. Same frustration. Completely different person in the room. If only you had had this framing in your first week, you said. Well, you have it now, and the first week is not the week that counts.

I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one.
I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement.
If only I had read the room, I would have caught the understatement.
I wish I were further along with the unwritten rules, but I am getting there.
I wish the meetings started on time, so I model the punctuality myself.
I wish the meetings got to the point faster, but the small talk is where the rapport is built, so I break the ice first.
I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one, because I read the formality of this office wrong.
I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement, so I now say let me think on that out loud.
If only I had read the room, I would have caught the understatement behind the word interesting.
The reciprocity here is real: I invest in the small talk first, and the team invests back. I am code-switching, not resisting.
